topic: Part 1 evidence-roadmap div — Tier 3 paragraph reframed per EXP-156/ISS-920 to lead with strength and drop apologetic framing
change_summary:
EXP-156 (ISS-920) replaced the Tier 3

in the evidence-roadmap div. Old: described kill-shots as relying on external datasets and pre-announced the dome defender's 'measured from the wrong frame' objection. New: names three concrete kill-shots (Gaia parallax ~1.8 billion stars, ~38 µs/day GPS correction, Southern Hemisphere great-circle distances), frames external datasets as 'independently decisive,' and preserves Tier 1 primacy via a closing clause.
our_argument_summary: The evidence-roadmap div now presents a three-tier argument structure: Tier 1 (self-contradictions from dome's own equations — Schumann, two-bulge tides), Tier 2 (per-WIN evaluation), Tier 3 (kill-shots using external data — Gaia, GPS, Southern Hemisphere distances). Tier 3's new final clause concedes that Tier 1 'goes one step further by needing no external data at all,' preserving the hierarchy logic while no longer pre-announcing a defender's escape hatch.

section_context.location: data/sections.json part1.html, div.evidence-roadmap (permanently visible, intentionally NOT wrapped in <details>)

holes_found.description: Factual imprecision inherited from Part 5: 'Gaia parallax of 1.8 billion stars.' Gaia DR3 catalogues ~1.811 billion sources, but only ~1.468 billion have published five-parameter astrometric (parallax) solutions. The remainder have two-parameter position-only solutions. A dome defender could pounce on this — 'your own showpiece overstates its own data by ~300 million stars' — to undermine the roadmap's authority at the earliest, most-read point of the document. The error is not introduced by EXP-156 (Part 5 and the KILLSHOT-GAIA text use the same figure), but EXP-156 propagates it into the orientation paragraph readers see first.
holes_found.recommendation: Either (a) replace '1.8 billion stars' with '~1.5 billion stars with measured parallax' in the roadmap AND in Part 5 and the Gaia kill-shot for consistency, or (b) keep '1.8 billion' but change 'parallax of' to 'astrometric survey of' — which is strictly accurate since Gaia's survey covers 1.8B sources even though not all have parallax. Option (b) requires fewer synchronous edits. A cross-file patch should propagate whichever fix is chosen.

holes_found.description: The phrase 'measurements the dome did not choose' is rhetorically odd and epistemologically weak. It reads as if we're arguing that the dome's objection to external data would be 'I didn't pick those datasets' — which isn't actually the dome's objection. The actual dome objection, which EXP-091 acknowledged, is that external datasets may be *theory-laden* in ways that presuppose a globe (parallax assumes a spherical orbit; GPS WGS84 is globe-based). 'Did not choose' does not address 'presupposes a globe.' The EXP-156 anticipated-objections response correctly identifies the theory-ladenness concern and rebuts it (Gaia measures angular shifts that a flat-disc must reproduce; GPS corrections are empirically necessary), but that rebuttal lives in the EXP file, not in the roadmap text. A defender reading only the roadmap sees 'did not choose' and can dismiss it.
holes_found.recommendation: Consider tightening the clause to something like 'These arguments rest on measurements that aren't derived from globe physics — they are raw angular shifts (Gaia), raw clock drift (GPS), and raw travel times (SH distances).' This directly addresses theory-ladenness without previewing the defender's objection. Alternatively, leave it alone but ensure Parts 5 and 7 contain the anti-theory-ladenness rebuttal explicitly.

holes_found.description: The roadmap cites '(Parts 5 and 7)' for Tier 3, but EXP-156's own metadata notes that Southern Hemisphere great-circle distances are addressed in 'Part 5 Test 7 and Part 4.' Part 4 is not listed in the Tier 3 parenthetical. A careful reader looking for the SH-distance kill-shot in Parts 5 and 7 will find it in Part 5 (13 sh_distance mentions) and Part 7 (6 mentions), but the primary Part 4 treatment is unlinked from this roadmap.
holes_found.recommendation: Either add 'and Part 4' to the parenthetical, or confirm that Part 4's SH-distance content is fully duplicated in Part 5 Test 7 (the kill-shot version). If Part 4 is the primary analytical treatment and Part 5 only references it, the roadmap should point readers to Part 4 too.

holes_found.description: The new Tier 3 text uses straight ASCII apostrophes ('dome's', 'dome did not choose') whereas the rest of the roadmap (and the EXP-156 replacement_html block) uses curly apostrophes (dome's, dome's). sections.json already holds straight apostrophes throughout part1.html for the reviewed passage, but the EXP-156 spec prescribed curly ones in replacement_html. Minor cosmetic inconsistency — not a correctness issue, but a typographic one that can be flagged to the decider.
holes_found.recommendation: Run a style sweep on part1.html to normalize apostrophe style across the entire evidence-roadmap div. Pick one convention (curly typographic or straight ASCII) and apply globally. Not urgent.

holes_found.description: Logical tension noted in EXP-156's own anticipated objections: 'Tier 3 is independently decisive' AND 'Tier 1 goes one step further' is a defensible combined claim, but the roadmap compresses it into a single paragraph without signposting the epistemic-strength axis clearly. A reader could reasonably ask: if Tier 3 is already decisive, what does 'going further' mean? The EXP-156 author's rebuttal ('Tier 1 decides without requiring any trust in external datasets — a strictly stronger epistemic position') is correct but lives only in the proposal package, not in the published text.
holes_found.recommendation: Consider adding one short clarifying clause, e.g., 'Tier 1 goes one step further by needing no external data at all — a strictly stronger epistemic position, which is why Parts 2 and 2b are placed before Parts 5 and 7.' This makes the hierarchy logic explicit for skeptical readers.

stronger_arguments.description: The roadmap could name a fourth exemplar at essentially zero cost: submarine-cable great-circle routings. Unlike Gaia (scientific), GPS (technical), and SH distances (flight data), submarine cables are operationally deployed by companies that must literally unroll thousands of km of fiber along a specific geodesic. They are the most concrete, least-disputable Tier-3 evidence the review has. A defender can theorize about 'wrong frame' for Gaia, but cable routes are industrial facts.
sources: Part 5 Test 7 / Part 4 content on SH distances

stronger_arguments.description: The Tier 1 examples are Schumann (~22 Hz vs 7.83 Hz observed) and two-bulge tides. Strong, but the Steelman Evidence Ladder could additionally cite the κ-denominator self-contradiction (WIN-012/013/014 mutual exclusion) as a pure arithmetic Tier 1 case — zero geometry, zero external data, just the dome's own numerical claims contradicting each other. That's the hardest kind of Tier 1 argument to escape because it doesn't even require a geometric model; it's just mathematics on the dome's own stated values. Adding one sentence would make Tier 1's claim to 'categorical strength' more convincing.

kernel_of_truth.description: EXP-156 correctly identified that the original Tier 3 paragraph was rhetorically self-undermining. Leading with 'kill-shot binary tests... rely on external datasets' and then conceding 'a determined defender could dispute as measured from the wrong frame' was a textbook case of showing the opposition your own sword before the duel. The new text is epistemologically equivalent (both versions acknowledge Tier 3 is weaker than Tier 1) but rhetorically much stronger — the reader now encounters three concrete, named datasets before any concession, and the concession itself is repositioned as 'Tier 1 is even stronger' rather than 'Tier 3 has a vulnerability.' EXP-156's kernel-of-truth commentary (that strength is communicated by naming concrete evidence, not previewing objections) is correct.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: N/A — this is a section-level review where the kernel-of-truth is about the EXP proposal itself, which we are endorsing. The only caveats are the moderate holes above (Gaia 1.8B figure, 'did not choose' phrasing) which don't undermine the reframe but do leave minor attack surface.

deeper_analysis:
I reviewed the reframed Tier 3 paragraph from four angles: (1) adversarial — what a dome defender would say; (2) factual — do the cited numbers match Parts 5 and 7; (3) consistency — does the new framing cohere with Tier 1 and Tier 2 descriptions; (4) completeness — does it over- or under-promise.

(1) Adversarial: The new text removes the two phrases a defender could most easily weaponize ('rely on external datasets', 'determined defender could dispute as measured from the wrong frame'). The remaining attack surface is narrower: the 'did not choose' phrasing (moderate) and the 1.8B Gaia figure (moderate if a defender Googles it). The 'independently decisive' claim is strong but defensible given Parts 5 and 7 content.

(2) Factual: 38 µs/day matches Part 7 (which cites +38.7 µs/day); 1.8 billion stars matches Part 5 and the KILLSHOT-GAIA kill-shot verbatim — so the roadmap is internally consistent with the rest of the review, even if both pieces share the mild imprecision about parallax-vs-survey. SH great-circle distances are in Parts 4, 5, and 7 as expected.

(3) Consistency: The new paragraph's closing clause ('Tier 1 self-contradictions in Parts 2 and 2b go one step further by needing no external data at all') mirrors the opening of the Tier 1 paragraph ('Arguments derived entirely from the dome's own stated parameters and equations'). No contradiction. The "This ordering is deliberate" closing paragraph is unchanged and continues to work.

(4) Completeness: The paragraph promises that Tier 3 'cannot be reproduced [by dome geometry] without rebuilding its own parameters.' That's a strong, testable claim. Parts 5 and 7 back it up (Gaia parallax has no dome analog because stars are at firmament height; GPS satellite clock corrections require 20,200 km altitude above a spherical mass; SH distances require a spherical Earth). So the promise is honored — but the roadmap is writing a check that Parts 5 and 7 must cash. If any of those three analyses weakens, the roadmap's Tier 3 credibility is undercut.

Final verdict: current_verdict_holds = true, recommended_action = minor_edit for the 1.8B figure and 'did not choose' phrasing. The reframe is a net improvement over the prior apologetic version.

citations_verified: Gaia DR3 mention — consistent with Part 5 Test 7 and KILLSHOT-GAIA content (both also cite 1.8 billion)
~38 µs/day GPS correction — Part 7 §7.2 cites +38.7 µs/day net (45.9 GR - 7.2 SR); the roadmap's '~38' is a correct rounding
Southern Hemisphere great-circle distances — Parts 5 and 7 both contain this content; Part 5 Test 7 is the primary kill-shot
citations_failed: Gaia DR3 parallax vs survey imprecision: 1.8 billion is the total source count; only ~1.468 billion have parallax solutions. This affects the roadmap, Part 5, and KILLSHOT-GAIA simultaneously. Not a DOI failure — a numerical-claim failure.
citations_unchecked: EXP-156's claim that GPS works 'empirically' without GR — not cited in the roadmap itself but present in Part 7; not re-verified in this review

advocate_mode_notes: Playing the dome defender: 'Your orientation section says Gaia measures 1.8 billion stellar parallaxes. Gaia DR3 has 1.811 billion sources but only 1.468 billion parallaxes. You're overstating your showpiece dataset by ~23%. If you can't get the numbers right in the roadmap, why should I trust the analyses downstream?' — This is the single most dangerous attack a defender could make on the reframed text, and it's inherited from Part 5, not introduced by EXP-156. Strongly recommend a cross-file patch to either 'survey of 1.8 billion stars' or 'parallax of ~1.5 billion stars' for global consistency.
cross_consistency_notes: The roadmap claims Tier 3 kill-shots appear in 'Parts 5 and 7'. Gaia is in Part 5 (✓), GPS is in Part 7 (✓), SH distances are in Parts 4, 5, and 7. The parenthetical under-counts Part 4; either add Part 4 to the parenthetical or confirm Part 5's treatment is authoritative.

gaia_1_8_billion_stars.actual: 1.811 billion sources (DR3); ~1.468 billion with parallax; ~1.592 billion with proper motions. Source: ESA Gaia DR3 summary.
gaia_1_8_billion_stars.verdict: misleading but widely used; matches Part 5 internal usage

gps_38_us_per_day.actual: +38.7 µs/day net (Part 7 §7.2 cites this exact figure)
